questionText: A point on a rotating wheel of radius $6$ m, centred at the origin $O$, has position modelled by $x = 6\cos t$ and $y = 6\sin t$. Find the equation of the normal to the point's path at the instant $t = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$, and show that this normal passes through the centre $O$.

steps.description: Differentiate x with respect to t
steps.explanation: Differentiating $6\cos t$ gives $-6\sin t$, the horizontal velocity of the point on the wheel.

steps.description: Differentiate y with respect to t
steps.explanation: Differentiating $6\sin t$ gives $6\cos t$, the vertical velocity.

steps.description: Form the gradient of the path
steps.explanation: Dividing the two rates and cancelling the $6$ leaves $-\cot t$, the slope of the circular path.

steps.description: Evaluate the tangent gradient at t = pi/6

steps.explanation: The normal is perpendicular to the tangent, so we take the negative reciprocal of $-\sqrt{3}$.

steps.description: Find the point at t = pi/6
steps.explanation: Substituting $t = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ into the position gives the point $\left(3\sqrt{3},\,3\right)$ that the normal passes through.

steps.description: Write the normal in point-slope form
steps.explanation: The normal passes through $\left(3\sqrt{3},\,3\right)$ with gradient $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, written in point-slope form.
The product simplifies neatly because $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}} = 3$, which will make the constants cancel.
The $-3$ and $+3$ cancel, leaving a line with zero constant term: $y = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}x$.
Substituting $x = 0$ gives $y = 0$, so the point $(0,\,0)$ lies on the normal.
Because the line has no constant term it passes through the origin, the centre $O$ — expected, since for a circle every normal points along a radius.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The normal at $t = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ is $y = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}x$ (equivalently $x - \sqrt{3}\,y = 0$); since its constant term is zero it passes through the centre $O(0,\,0)$.
